note: FROZEN DEVELOPMENT SET. The operational-synonym layer was tuned while observing failures on these exact prompts, so results here are post-tuning dev accuracy, NOT independent evidence of generalization. Measure generalization on clickstack_prompts_holdout.json instead.

prompts.text: Investigate rising HTTP latency alongside Redis evictions, memory use, and connected clients.
Chart request duration, cache hit and miss behavior, Redis memory pressure, evictions, and client pressure.
Find whether Redis memory exhaustion and client saturation explain the API latency increase.
Correlate p95 request latency with cache size, misses, evicted keys, used memory, and blocked clients.
Build an incident view for HTTP duration and Redis cache capacity, eviction, memory, and connections.
Check cache hit ratio, misses and evictions against request latency and Redis client load.
Checkout is slow. Show me what is under pressure.
Something around the payment cache looks unhealthy.
Why did response times get bad during the traffic spike?
The service degraded. Look at the cache and its dependencies.
Users are waiting longer and Redis might be involved.
Investigate the checkout slowdown without assuming the cause.
SEV2 checkout latency up after lunch; deploy looked normal, Kafka had a blip, Redis memory and clients seem weird.
Payments slow, no clear 5xx jump, CPU okay, cache misses and evictions may have moved.
Customer reports timeouts; pods are stable but cache size, memory, and connections changed around 14:05.
Could be network, JVM, or Redis. Compare request duration with cache pressure and client load.
Traffic rose 20%, latency doubled, deployment unchanged; inspect Redis hit rate, memory, evictions, and blocked clients.
Checkout red, auth green, database mostly green, Redis yellow. Find the useful latency and cache signals.
This looks like CPU saturation, but verify whether cache evictions or Redis clients better explain the latency.
Assume the database is slow, then challenge that assumption using request and cache evidence.
The alert says network loss; check whether memory pressure in Redis is the actual bottleneck.
A deploy happened nearby, but test the cache-pressure hypothesis before blaming the release.
Errors are flat, so this cannot be Redis. Prove or disprove that with latency, eviction, and client metrics.
Kafka lag is noisy. Focus only if it explains the checkout latency better than cache pressure.
Did key churn and connection load make the request path take longer?
Compare response-time deterioration with object retention, removals, and Redis consumer load.
Was the fast-data layer squeezed when checkout duration climbed?
Relate slower transactions to key removals, working-set growth, and open Redis connections.
Show whether the in-memory tier ran out of headroom as requests slowed.
Investigate response delay through reuse efficiency, discarded entries, memory headroom, and connection demand.
